{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01G5R6fbcDBCmA2XrsFJvGCL","type":"tool_result","content":"<persisted-output>\nOutput too large (29.9KB). Full output saved to: /logs/agent/sessions/projects/-app/4c2a3f2b-0408-4939-a2aa-89cf838c713d/tool-results/b913068.txt\n\nPreview (first 2KB):\n# A Genome-Wide Association Study Confirms VKORC1, CYP2C9, and CYP4F2 as Principal Genetic Determinants of Warfarin Dose\nWe report the first genome-wide association study (GWAS) whose sample size (1,053 Swedish subjects) is sufficiently powered to detect genome-wide significance (p<1.5×10−7) for polymorphisms that modestly alter therapeutic warfarin dose. The anticoagulant drug warfarin is widely prescribed for reducing the risk of stroke, thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, and coronary malfunction. However, Caucasians vary widely (20-fold) in the dose needed for therapeutic anticoagulation, and hence prescribed doses may be too low (risking serious illness) or too high (risking severe bleeding). Prior work established that ∼30% of the dose variance is explained by single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the warfarin drug target VKORC1 and another ∼12% by two non-synonymous SNPs (*2, *3) in the cytochrome P450 warfarin-metabolizing gene CYP2C9. We initially tested each of 325,997 GWAS SNPs for association with warfarin dose by univariate regression and found the strongest statistical signals (p<10−78) at SNPs clustering near VKORC1 and the second lowest p-values (p<10−31) emanating from CYP2C9. No other SNPs approached genome-wide significance. To enhance detection of weaker effects, we conducted multiple regression adjusting for known influences on warfarin dose (VKORC1, CYP2C9, age, gender) and identified a single SNP (rs2108622) with genome-wide significance (p = 8.3×10−10) that alters protein coding of the CYP4F2 gene. We confirmed this result in 588 additional Swedish patients (p<0.0029) and, during our investigation, a second group provided independent confirmation from a scan of warfarin-metabolizing genes. We also thoroughly investigated copy number variations, haplotypes, and imputed SNPs, but found no additional highly significant warfarin associations. We present power analysis of our GWAS that is generalizable to other studies, and conclude we had 80% pow\n...\n</persisted-output>","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01UdWWP6djz4H86YdYYju1dw","session_id":"4c2a3f2b-0408-4939-a2aa-89cf838c713d","uuid":"41057952-d416-4a1e-9b8d-405a6db869c5"}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01Wsnyt7CdpuA3w86k7zeeft","type":"tool_result","content":"# A Genome-Wide Association Study Confirms VKORC1, CYP2C9, and CYP4F2 as Principal Genetic Determinants of Warfarin Dose\nWe report the first genome-wide association study (GWAS) whose sample size (1,053 Swedish subjects) is sufficiently powered to detect genome-wide significance (p<1.5×10−7) for polymorphisms that modestly alter therapeutic warfarin dose. The anticoagulant drug warfarin is widely prescribed for reducing the risk of stroke, thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, and coronary malfunction. However, Caucasians vary widely (20-fold) in the dose needed for therapeutic anticoagulation, and hence prescribed doses may be too low (risking serious illness) or too high (risking severe bleeding). Prior work established that ∼30% of the dose variance is explained by single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the warfarin drug target VKORC1 and another ∼12% by two non-synonymous SNPs (*2, *3) in the cytochrome P450 warfarin-metabolizing gene CYP2C9. We initially tested each of 325,997 GWAS SNPs for association with warfarin dose by univariate regression and found the strongest statistical signals (p<10−78) at SNPs clustering near VKORC1 and the second lowest p-values (p<10−31) emanating from CYP2C9. No other SNPs approached genome-wide significance. To enhance detection of weaker effects, we conducted multiple regression adjusting for known influences on warfarin dose (VKORC1, CYP2C9, age, gender) and identified a single SNP (rs2108622) with genome-wide significance (p = 8.3×10−10) that alters protein coding of the CYP4F2 gene. We confirmed this result in 588 additional Swedish patients (p<0.0029) and, during our investigation, a second group provided independent confirmation from a scan of warfarin-metabolizing genes. We also thoroughly investigated copy number variations, haplotypes, and imputed SNPs, but found no additional highly significant warfarin associations. We present power analysis of our GWAS that is generalizable to other studies, and conclude we had 80% power to detect genome-wide significance for common causative variants or markers explaining at least 1.5% of dose variance. These GWAS results provide further impetus for conducting large-scale trials assessing patient benefit from genotype-based forecasting of warfarin dose.\nWe and others have shown that the warfarin drug target *VKORC1* (vitamin K epoxide reductase complex, subunit 1) contains common polymorphisms that account for a major portion (∼30%) of the variance in required warfarin dose [[5]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius2),[[6]](#pgen.1000433-DAndrea1), and we have recently evaluated ∼1500 Swedish patients of the Warfarin Genetics (WARG) cohort in the largest study to date showing likely patient benefit from genetic forecasting of dose [[3]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius1). The study confirmed that SNPs in *VKORC1* and in the warfarin-metabolizing gene *CYP2C9* (cytochrome P450, family 2, subfamily C, polypeptide 9) predict ∼40% of dose variance while non-genetic factors (age, sex, etc.) jointly account for another ∼15%. The robust and now widely replicated associations of warfarin dose with *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9* have provided one of the most successful applications of pharmacogenetics to date [[7]](#pgen.1000433-Rettie1) and offer promise for genetic predication of required dose in a clinical setting [[3]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius1).\nKnowledge of major predictors of warfarin dose also impacts the methodology for finding further dose-related genes. In early candidate gene work with a small sample of 201 patients [[8]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius3), we noted that univariate regression (with tested SNP as the only dose predictor) could statistically detect warfarin association with *VKORC1* and with one of two non-synonymous *CYP2C9* SNPs (**3*) known to influence warfarin dose ([Table 1](#pgen-1000433-t001) in [[8]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius3)). However, a second non-synonymous *CYP2C9* SNP (**2*) with known but weaker influence on warfarin dose was not detected by univariate regression, but **2* was statistically significant in multivariate regression adjusted for the other known genetic and non-genetic predictors of dose ([Table 3](#pgen-1000433-t003) in [[8]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius3)). These empirical results in a small warfarin sample provided a signpost underscoring the potential importance of multivariate regression for detecting weak effects in studies now searching for additional warfarin genes across the genome.\n|   | VKORC1 | CYP2C9*3 | CYP2C9*2 | CYP4F2 | Distribution of all SNPs |\n| Age, sex, VKORC1, CYP2C9*3 | – | – | 1.4E-26 | 8.3E-08 | – |\n| Age, Sex, VKORC1, CYP2C9*3 and *2 | – | – | – | 8.3E-10 | Figure 1B |\n|   |   | VKORC1 | CYP2C9*3 | CYP2C9*2 | CYP4F2 | Unknown | Unknown |\n| Age, sex, VKORC1, CYP2C9*3 | 0.532 | – | – | 1.00 | 0.40 | 0.73 | 0.31 |\n| Age, Sex, VKORC1, CYP2C9*3 and *2 | 0.570 | – | – | – | 0.48 | 0.81 | 0.39 |\n| Age, Sex, VKORC1, CYP2C9*3 and *2, CYP4F2 | 0.580 | – | – | – | – | 0.82 | 0.41 |\n[Figure 1A](#pgen-1000433-g001) and the first line of [Table 1](#pgen-1000433-t001) summarize results of testing 325,997 GWAS SNPs for association with warfarin dose by univariate regression. The strongest associations were at multiple SNPs in and near *VKORC1* ([Figure 1A](#pgen-1000433-g001)) with the lowest p-value given by rs9923231 (*P* = 5.4×10^−78^). In prior fine-mapping of the *VKORC1* locus [[8]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius1), we identified rs9923231 as one of three SNPs located in introns or immediately flanking *VKORC1* that exhibit almost perfectly concordant genotypes yielding pairwise linkage disequilibrium (LD) *r* ^2^≈1 and which define the warfarin-sensitive A-T-T haplotype at rs9923231-rs9934438-rs2359612 (see also [[11]](#pgen.1000433-Rieder1)). These highly concordant SNPs were the best predictors of warfarin dose in our previous study and in this GWAS analysis (p<5.4×10^−78^) and completely accounted for the dose variance explained by all other fine-mapping SNPs near *VKORC1* [[8]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius3). The group of SNPs with the second lowest univariate p-values clustered around *CYP2C9* which contains two non-synonymous exonic SNPs whose minor alleles (**2*, **3*) impair warfarin metabolism and are well known to be associated with warfarin dose. In our previous work [[8]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius3), we discovered an unusual SNP (rs4917639) whose minor allele is almost perfectly associated with the “composite” *CYP2C9* allele formed by combining **2* and **3* into a single allele. Indeed, the GWAS results (1053 subjects) confirmed that LD is nearly perfect (pairwise *r* ^2^≈1.0) between rs4917639 and the composite of **2* and **3*. Thus, the highly significant univariate result for rs4917639 (*R* ^2^ = 0.121, p<3.1×10^−31^) reflects the combined effect of *CYP2C9*2* rs1799853 (*R* ^2^ = 0.038, p<8.8×10^−13^) and *CYP2C9*3* rs1057910 (*R* ^2^ = 0.080, p<4.5×10^−17^). [Figure 1A](#pgen-1000433-g001) therefore indicates p-values for this composite SNP as well as for **2* and **3*.\nHorizontal axis shows SNP location and vertical axis is −log10(p-value) for each SNP tested by univariate regression (A) or multivariate regression (B). Red dots and red lettering show SNPs and implicated genes with p-values beyond the genome-wide significance threshold (1.5×10−7) which is denoted by a horizontal line. (A) Univariate regression shows genome-wide significant association to SNPs clustering near the warfarin drug target VKORC1 (e.g., P = 5.4×10−78, rs9923231) and near the warfarin-metabolizing gene CYP2C9 (P = 4.5×10−17 for non-synonymous *3 SNP rs1057910, P = 8.8×10−13 for non-synonymous *2 SNP rs1799853, P = 3.1×10−31 for *2*3 “composite” SNP rs4917639). (B) Multivariate regression adjusting for the contributions of VKORC1 and CYP2C9 had greater power than univariate regression and detected genome-wide significant association to the CYP4F2 gene (P = 8.3×10−10, non-synonymous SNP rs2108622).\n[Figure 1B](#pgen-1000433-g001) and [Table 1](#pgen-1000433-t001) (lines 2 to 5) show the results of multivariate regression analysis in which individual SNPs were tested for association with warfarin dose after adjustment for established genetic and non-genetic predictors of dose. The only SNP reaching genome-wide significance (p<1.5×10^−7^) was a non-synonymous SNP (rs2108622) in exon 2 of *CYP4F2* (cytochrome P450, family 4, subfamily F, polypeptide 2) introducing a Val to Met amino acid change at position 433 (V433M). SNP rs2108622 predicts additional dose variance (∼1.1%) that is independent of the variance already explained by *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9*. As noted in the Introduction, our early studies with a small sample of 201 Swedish patients failed to detect the weak *CYP2C9*2* effect on dose by univariate regression but **2* was significant in multiple regression [[8]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius3). The results in [Table 1](#pgen-1000433-t001) with rs2108622 of *CYP4F2* show the same phenomenon with a p-value of 1.6×10^−5^ in univariate regression (line 1) but progressively lower p-values as known predictors are added to the multivariate model so that for the full model a p-value of 8.3×10^−10^ is achieved which is far below genome-wide significance (p<1.5×10^−7^). The *CYP4F2* association was further confirmed by testing an independent replication panel of 588 Swedish warfarin patients who gave a multivariate p-value of 0.0029 and a total overall p-value of 3.3×10^−10^ when combined with the GWAS subjects ([Table 2](#pgen-1000433-t002)). During preparation of this paper, a candidate gene study of drug-metabolizing and transporter genes independently discovered the association of rs2108622 and *CYP4F2* with warfarin dose, providing further confirmation [[12]](#pgen.1000433-Caldwell1).\n| CYP2C9*3 rs1057910 (Ile359Leu, 0.070)a | −1.13 (−1.26, −1.00) | 0.075 | 2.6E-55 | −1.08 (−1.27, −0.89) | 0.089 | 2.3E-26 | −1.11 (−1.22, −1.00) | 0.080 | 2.6E-79 |\n| CYP2C9*2 rs1799853 (Arg144Cys, 0.109)a | −0.63 (−0.74, −0.52) | 0.048 | 1.7E-28 | −0.40 (−0.55, −0.24) | 0.023 | 5.5E-07 | −0.54 (−0.63, −0.45) | 0.038 | 1.1E-31 |\nTo increase the power of our multivariate regression model and possibly detect additional weak effects, we added *CYP4F2* (rs2108622) to the model as a predictor and conducted further analyses. First, we retested the GWAS SNPs, but no new SNPs reached genome-wide significance and there was also no apparent excess of SNPs at lower significance thresholds ([Figure S1](#pgen.1000433.s001)). We also tested warfarin association with haplotypes and with ungenotyped SNPs imputed at 2.2 million HapMap SNPs, but no haplotype or imputed SNP approached genome-wide significance in a genomic region not containing *VKORC1*, *CYP2C9* or *CYP4F2*. To explore whether copy number variations (CNVs) detectable by the HumanCNV370 array might influence warfarin dose, we used rigorous quality control and retained 879 samples calling 2530 CNVs (see [Materials and Methods](#s4)). None of the CNV loci were significantly associated with dose after correction for multiple testing (lowest CNV p-value was 1.1×10^−4^ which exceeds 0.05/2530≈2.0×10^−5^). We note that probe density in many of the detected CNVs is not optimal for conducting association analyses and these results should therefore be viewed as preliminary.\nFinally, after excluding SNPs near *VKORC1*, *CYP2C9* and *CYP4F2*, we identified 40 other loci containing one or more GWAS SNPs with p-values below 2.0×10^−4^ and we genotyped 40 SNPs representing these loci in a follow-up sample of 588 Swedish warfarin patients. However none of the 40 loci replicated for association with warfarin dose, the lowest p-value being 0.04 which is not significant after correction for 40 tests ([Table S1](#pgen.1000433.s003)). Having not found evidence for any additional genetic modulators of dose, we examined the entire data set (GWAS plus followup samples) for evidence of statistical interaction between pairs of the established dose predictors (*VKORC1, CYP2C9, CYP4F2*, age, sex). None of the pairs exhibited statistically significant interaction after p-values were corrected for the 15 interaction tests ([Table S2](#pgen.1000433.s004)).\nWe also performed a GWAS for a secondary trait (“over-anticoagulation”) which we previously found was associated with *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9* in a candidate gene study [[3]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius1). By titrating warfarin dose, physicians attempt to achieve a target level of anticoagulation determined by a reading of 2.0 to 3.0 for the prothrombin international normalized ratio (INR), which is the ratio of time required for a patient's blood to coagulate relative to that of a reference sample. However over-anticoagulation (defined as an INR above 4.0) sometimes occurs and, using Cox regression, our GWAS tested for SNP association with the occurrence of over-anticoagulation in patients during the first 5 weeks of treatment (see [Materials and Methods](#s4): Association testing of SNPs and haplotypes). We observed genome-wide significant association (p<1.5×10^−7^) at several SNPs in and around *VKORC1* including rs9923231 (P = 8.9×10^−9^), but no other SNPs achieved genome-wide significance including *CYP2C9*3* (p<4.0×10^−5^), *CYP2C9*2* (p = 0.93), or the “composite” **2*3* SNP rs4917639 (p<0.007) ([Figure S2](#pgen.1000433.s002)). However we note that our previous candidate gene study evaluated a larger sample set (1496 WARG subjects) which yielded genome-wide significant association with over-anticoagulation for both *VKORC1* rs9923231 (*P* = 5.7×10^−11^) and *CYP2C9*3* (*P* = 1.5×10^−9^) [[3]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius1). To explore whether these SNPs might cause over-anticoagulation independent of altering the required (i.e., administered) warfarin dose, we added required dose to the Cox regression model as a predictor of over-anticoagulation, and found that both *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9*3* have a significant effect independent of dose (*P*<0.05) ([Table S3](#pgen.1000433.s005)).\nWe conducted the first GWAS sufficiently powered to detect DNA variants with a modest influence on the warfarin dose needed to achieve therapeutic anticoagulation. In univariate analysis of GWAS SNPs ([Figure 1A](#pgen-1000433-g001)), we identified extremely strong association signals (p = 10^−78^ to 10^−13^) at SNPs in and near *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9*, two genes already known to explain ∼30% and ∼12% of warfarin dose variance, respectively. By applying multivariate regression adjusting for known genetic and non-genetic predictors of dose ([Figure 1B](#pgen-1000433-g001)), we also detected genome-wide significance of p<8.3×10^−10^ at *CYP4F2* (rs2108622) that accounted for approximately 1.5% of dose variance. The increased power of multivariate regression to detect this modest effect is nicely illustrated in [Table 1](#pgen-1000433-t001) which shows a higher univariate p-value for *CYP4F2* (p<1.6×10^−5^) but progressively lower multivariate p-values as known predictors of dose are added to the regression model. We confirmed the *CYP4F2* association in a second large sample set and the association was also reported by another group [[12]](#pgen.1000433-Caldwell1) during preparation of our work, thus fully establishing the genuine effect of *CYP4F2* (see also [[10]](#pgen.1000433-Cooper1) where *CYP4F2* explained ∼1% dose variance with nominal p<0.043 significance). Although multivariate regression has not been widely used to increase power in other GWAS analyses because known genetic variants usually explain little phenotypic variance, the potential for power increase is perhaps obvious if known predictors *do* explain substantial variance. Thus multiple regression has, for example, been previously advocated for linkage analyses of line crosses [[13]](#pgen.1000433-Haley1),[[14]](#pgen.1000433-Jansen1).\nTo estimate the multivariate regression power of our GWAS (1053 subjects), we used Equation 1 (see [Materials and Methods](#s4)) to calculate power to detect SNPs explaining specific magnitudes of variance () for warfarin dose (see [Table 3](#pgen-1000433-t003)). The table shows that power to achieve genome-wide significance (p<1.5×10^−7^) is essentially 100% for *VKORC1* rs9923231 (), *CYP2C9*3* () and *CYP2C9*2* (), but power falls to ∼48% for *CYP4F2* rs2108622 (). The table also shows that when *CYP4F2* is added to the multivariate model, a SNP accounting for 1.5% or 1.0% of the dose variance would have ∼82% or ∼41% power of being detected, respectively. Therefore we estimate that our GWAS had at least 80% power to detect warfarin-associated variants explaining at least 1.5% of the dose variance but 40% or less power to detect genome-wide significance if a variant accounts for 1% or less dose variance.\nHowever it is important to emphasize that these power estimates assume that the dose-altering DNA variant is genotyped and tested directly or is indirectly detected through a marker in sufficiently high LD to the dose variant that the marker's magnitude is detectable ([Table 3](#pgen-1000433-t003)). The assumption of directly testing the dose-altering variant is accurate for *CYP2C9*2* and **3* which are each known to alter warfarin metabolism [[15]](#pgen.1000433-Stubbins1),[[16]](#pgen.1000433-Aithal1) and is likely correct for *CYP4F2* rs2108622 which, like *2 and *3, changes protein coding sequence. However, to explore whether other dose-altering variants might be undetected due to insufficient LD with genotyped GWAS SNPs, we determined the relationship between the variance observed at a marker () and at the causative variant () assuming pairwise LD of *r* ^2^ between the two polymorphisms (see [Materials and Methods](#s4): How Much Does Linkage Disequilibrium Attenuate Association with a Quantitative Trait?). The relationship is given by Equation 3 in [Materials and Methods](#s4) () which is analogous to Pritchard and Prezworski's relationship () for the number of cases () providing equal power in a case-control study that tests either the disease-causing SNP or a nearby marker [[17]](#pgen.1000433-Pritchard1). To use the equation to estimate magnitudes for variants that might be *undetected* by our GWAS, we note that ∼90% of the GWAS SNPs had a minor allele frequency (MAF) above 10% in our warfarin subjects implying that a “rare” dose-altering variant (MAF≈1%–5%) would be covered at a likely maximum *r* ^2^ of only ∼0.1 to ∼0.5. This low *r* ^2^ coverage implies that rare variants could have values (0.05 to 0.02) easily detected by regression testing of the variant itself, but unlikely to be detected through a GWAS marker since maximum values could drop to 0.01 or much lower (see Equation 3 and [Table 3](#pgen-1000433-t003)). By contrast, “common” SNPs (MAF≥5%), which might also be dose variants, are covered by GWAS SNPs of this study at reasonably high *r* ^2^ values in most instances (*r* ^2^>0.8 or *r* ^2^>0.5 for ∼60% or ∼80% respectively of common SNPs [[18]](#pgen.1000433-Bhangale1) and *r* ^2^>0.9 for ∼90% of non-synonymous common SNPs [[19]](#pgen.1000433-Evans1) in HapMap Caucasians). We therefore conclude that our GWAS probably detected most common SNP variants explaining 1.5% or more of the warfarin dose variance, but may have failed to detect rarer variants that could individually explain up to 5% of dose variance. We further note that the HumanCNV370 array used in this study does not have the required marker complement to undertake a comprehensive GWAS of common CNVs.\nAs noted in the Introduction, the widely replicated warfarin dose associations with *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9* represent one of the most successful applications of pharmacogenetics to date. Our study together with that of Caldwell *et al*. [[12]](#pgen.1000433-Caldwell1) now also clearly demonstrates that *CYP4F2* (rs2108622) is a third gene that influences warfarin dose, but our GWAS and statistical analysis also implies that additional common SNP variants that influence dose may not exist in Caucasian populations. However, Caucasians might carry common variants with effects smaller than *CYP4F2* or rare variants whose effects are substantially larger than the ∼1% of dose variance explained by *CYP4F2*. Furthermore, other unidentified genes may influence warfarin dose in other ethnicities such as Asians or Africans, and some rare dose-altering variants in known genes such as *VKORC1* may exist in only a subset of populations of European descent [[20]](#pgen.1000433-Scott1). Hence, future research could address ethnic differences in the genetic variants that influence warfarin dose as well as subtle intra-ethnic differences and admixture that may exist in European or other populations.\nIn a recent study [[3]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius1), we highlighted the potential benefit of pre-treatment forecasting of required warfarin dose based on patient genotypes at *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9* together with non-genetic predictors of dose. Indeed, in August 2007, the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA) updated warfarin labeling to recommend initiating lower warfarin dose in some patients based on *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9* genotypes. However this recommendation is not a requirement due to a lack of large trials demonstrating warfarin patient benefit from dose forecasting (though two small trials [[21]](#pgen.1000433-Anderson1),[[22]](#pgen.1000433-Caraco1) do support such benefit; see also [[23]](#pgen.1000433-Lenzini1)–[[27]](#pgen.1000433-Hillman1) for reviews and other trials). The results of our GWAS provide further impetus for conducting large-scale dose-forecasting trials by identifying *CYP4F2* as a third genetic predictor of dose and also by showing that additional major genetic predictors may not exist in Caucasians or may not emerge in the near-term. Hence, large-scale trials of patient benefit from dose forecasting based on *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9* (with possible inclusion of *CYP4F2* as a minor predictor) are likely to provide state-of-the-art clinical benchmarks for warfarin use during the foreseeable future.\nFrom approximately 1500 WARG samples [[3]](#pgen.1000433-Wadelius1) examined for non-degradation and appropriate concentration of DNA (∼50 ng/µl), we randomly selected 1208 subjects for genotyping SNPs and CNV probes using the HumanCNV370 BeadChip array (Illumina). We excluded SNPs with MAF below 1%, call rate below 95%, or if call rate fell below 99% when MAF was below 5%. SNPs that departed from Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (P<10^−6^) were also excluded. Subjects with genotyping call rate below 95% were also eliminated. Using iPLEX (Sequenom), subject identity (and associated phenotypic data) was cross-checked by genotyping four gender markers and 47 SNPs also carried on the HumanCNV370 array, enabling us to exclude ∼136 misidentified subjects. Sample quality (contamination) was further assessed by plotting each subject's genome-wide heterozygosity and eliminating outliers (with heterozygosity above or below the range of 0.312–0.372). After these quality control steps, a total of 1053 warfarin patients and 325,997 GWAS SNPs were retained for analysis. The GWAS SNPs included two SNPs not on the HumanCNV370 array but which are highly predictive of warfarin dose [rs9923231 (*VKORC1*) and rs1799853 (*CYP2C9*2*)] which we genotyped by TaqMan assay (Applied Biosystems).\nWe tested for statistical interaction in modulating warfarin dose for each pair of established dose predictors (*VKORC1* rs9923231, *CYP2C9**2 and *3, *CYP4F2* rs2108622, Age, Sex) using multivariate regression and R software as described above. An interaction term formed by multiplying the pair of predictor variables was added to the multivariate regression equation which contained only main effects of the 6 predictors, and standard ANOVA compared this main-effect model with the enhanced interaction model by testing for a statistically significant increase in explained dose variance. Interaction test p-values were considered statistically significant if below the Bonferroni cutpoint determined by correcting for the 15 interaction tests (i.e. p<0.0033≈0.05/15).\nTo possibly identify SNPs with genuine but weak associations to warfarin dose, we excluded *VKORC1*, *CYP2C9*, *CYP4F2* and identified 40 other GWAS loci for follow-up genotyping exhibiting multivariate regression p-values below 0.0002, and selected 40 SNPs representing these loci for genotyping. Only genotyped (not imputed) SNPs were chosen for follow-up. We genotyped the same 558 patients as in the *CYP4F2* replication using the iPLEX MassARRAY.\n2. Takahashi H, Echizen H. Pharmacogenetics of CYP2C9 and interindividual variability in anticoagulant response to warfarin. Pharmacogenomics J. 2003;3:202–214. doi: 10.1038/sj.tpj.6500182. {"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01EYSkb6GTvCbQYJ2VuQxeZY","type":"tool_result","content":"# Absorption of Montelukast is Transporter Mediated: a Common Variant of OATP2B1 is Associated with Reduced Plasma Concentrations and Poor Response\n(i) determine if montelukast undergoes carrier mediated uptake; (ii) classify the carrier protein(s) responsible for uptake; (iii) identify specific transporters that mediate transport of montelukast; (iv) evaluate whether variation in the gene encoding the transport protein(s) influences the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of montelukast.\nIn vitro permeability studies of montelukast are conducted using Caco-2 cell culture, a standard model of human intestinal drug transport. In vivo plasma concentrations of montelukast in an asthmatic population are determined by HPLC, and genotyping of transport proteins is by LightTyper analysis.\nPermeability of montelukast has an activation energy of 13.7±0.7 kcal/mol, consistent with carrier mediated transport. Permeability is saturable at high concentrations of montelukast and follows Michaelis-Menten kinetics. Permeability is subject to competition by sulfobromophthalein, estrone 3-sulfate, pravastatin, taurocholic acid, and cholic acid (p<0.05, % of control; 72±7 – 86±7) and is inhibited by 5–10% citrus juice (p<0.05, maximal inhibition % of control; 31±2). An MDCKII cell line expressing OATP2B1 (coded for by the SLCO2B1 gene) displays significantly increased permeability of montelukast (p<0.05, % of control; 140±20). A nonsynonymous polymorphism in SLCO2B1, (rs12422149; SLCO2B1{NM_007256.2}:c.935G>A) associates with significantly reduced plasma concentration in subjects measured on the morning after an evening dose (p<0.025, square root mean transformed plasma concentration ± SE; c.[935G>A]+[935G]=3±1, c.[935G]+[935G]=7.0±0.9) and differential response as assessed by change in baseline Asthma Symptom Utility Index scores following one month of therapy (delta mean Asthma Symptom Utility Index; c.[935G>A]+[935G] = 0.02±0.01, p=1.0; c.[935G]+[935G] = 1.0±0.3, p<0.0001).\nTogether these observations suggest that the genetics of SLCO2B1 may be an important variable in determining the pharmacokinetics and the pharmacodynamics of montelukast.\nKeywords: Asthma, human OATP2B1 protein, intestinal absorption, leukotriene antagonists, membrane transport proteins, montelukast, pharmacodynamics, pharmacokinetics, single nucleotide polymorphism\n(i) determine if montelukast undergoes carrier mediated uptake; (ii) classify the carrier protein(s) responsible for uptake; (iii) identify specific transporters that mediate transport of montelukast; (iv) evaluate whether variation in the gene encoding the transport protein(s) influences the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of montelukast.\nIn vitro permeability studies of montelukast are conducted using Caco-2 cell culture, a standard model of human intestinal drug transport. In vivo plasma concentrations of montelukast in an asthmatic population are determined by HPLC, and genotyping of transport proteins is by LightTyper analysis.\nPermeability of montelukast has an activation energy of 13.7±0.7 kcal/mol, consistent with carrier mediated transport. Permeability is saturable at high concentrations of montelukast and follows Michaelis-Menten kinetics. Permeability is subject to competition by sulfobromophthalein, estrone 3-sulfate, pravastatin, taurocholic acid, and cholic acid (p<0.05, % of control; 72±7 – 86±7) and is inhibited by 5–10% citrus juice (p<0.05, maximal inhibition % of control; 31±2). An MDCKII cell line expressing OATP2B1 (coded for by the *SLCO2B1* gene) displays significantly increased permeability of montelukast (p<0.05, % of control; 140±20). A nonsynonymous polymorphism in *SLCO2B1*, (rs12422149; *SLCO2B1*{[NM_007256.2](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/nuccore/NM_007256.2)}:c.935G>A) associates with significantly reduced plasma concentration in subjects measured on the morning after an evening dose (p<0.025, square root mean transformed plasma concentration ± SE; c.[935G>A]+[935G]=3±1, c.[935G]+[935G]=7.0±0.9) and differential response as assessed by change in baseline Asthma Symptom Utility Index scores following one month of therapy (delta mean Asthma Symptom Utility Index; c.[935G>A]+[935G] = 0.02±0.01, p=1.0; c.[935G]+[935G] = 1.0±0.3, p<0.0001).\nTogether these observations suggest that the genetics of *SLCO2B1* may be an important variable in determining the pharmacokinetics and the pharmacodynamics of montelukast.\nMontelukast (Singulair™, Merck & Co., Inc., Whitehouse Station, NJ) is a leukotriene receptor antagonist (LTRA) that is available as a once-daily oral formulation used to control symptoms associated with asthma in children and adults. Montelukast selectively inhibits binding of Cys-LTD4, a potent mediator of airway inflammation and bronchoconstriction, to the cysteinyl leukotriene 1 (Cys-LT1) receptor [[1](#R1)]. Cys-LTs are synthesized from arachidonic acid released from membrane-phospholipids by cytosolic phospholipase A2 (cPLA2) through the action of 5-lipoxygenase and FLAP in response to stimulation [[2](#R2), [3](#R3)].\nNumerous clinical trials have established the efficacy and safety of montelukast [[4](#R4)–[6](#R6)], however the high inter-individual variability in response, both in children and adults, limits the drug’s effectiveness [[7](#R7), [8](#R8)]. It has been estimated that as much as 60 to 80% of the variability in response to asthma drugs may be due to genetic variation [[9](#R9)]. We and others have reported associations between response to leukotriene receptor antagonists and polymorphisms in leukotriene pathway genes [[10](#R10)–[14](#R14)], suggesting that these genetic variants are important contributors to the variability in pharmacodynamic responsiveness to leukotriene receptor antagonists (LTRAs).\nThe physical and chemical properties of montelukast disfavor absorption by passive diffusion [[15](#R15)]. For example, montelukast is a weak acid with a pKa of 5.7 [[16](#R16)] indicating that the drug is partially ionized at intestinal pH. Montelukast has a high molecular weight (MW = 586.2 g/mol), and is strongly lipophilic (calculated logarithm of the octanol:water partition coefficient = 8.98) suggesting that once partitioned into the cell membrane its release would be disfavored [[17](#R17)]. Montelukast is highly bound to plasma albumin and is 64% bioavailable [[18](#R18)]-- characteristics shared by oral drugs whose transport is mediated at least in part by membrane transport proteins [[19](#R19)].\nThe aims of the present study were to: (i) determine if montelukast undergoes carrier mediated uptake; (ii) classify the carrier protein(s) that may be responsible for uptake of the drug; and (iii) identify specific transporters that contribute to transport of montelukast. Finally, to determine the clinical relevance of carrier mediated transport; (iv) evaluate whether variation in the gene(s) encoding the transport protein(s) influence the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of montelukast.\nWe report that montelukast undergoes carrier mediated transport by both OATP2B1 and OATP1A2 (coded for by the *SLCO2B1* and *SLCO1A2* genes, respectively) and that genetic variation in *SLCO2B1* contributes to variability in its pharmacokinetics and therapeutic response in subjects with asthma.\nMontelukast, sodium salt was purchased from LKT Laboratories, Inc., St. Paul Minnesota and was tritiated to a specific activity of 5 Ci/mmol by exchange (Moravek Biochemicals, Inc, Brea California). The radiochemical purity as assessed by C18 reversed phase HPLC was 97.2%. Other compounds were obtained from the following suppliers: chenodeoxycholic acid, sodium salt, MK571, sodium salt, and taurocholic acid, sodium salt were from Calbiochem; deoxycholic acid, sodium salt was from Difco; cholic acid, sodium salt was from Research Organics; DL-buthionine sulfoximine, bumetanide, cimetidine, 4,4'-Diisothiocyanostilbene-2,2'-disulfonic acid (DIDS), estrone 3-sulfate, sodium salt, ethacrynic acid, folic acid, furosemide, genistein, pravastatin, probenecid, progesterone, sulfobromophthalein, tamoxifen, thiamine, hydrochloride, and verapamil were from Sigma. Orange juice (lot: 31001 701820427 NC CT803) and white grapefruit juice (lot: 12-500 1-03:40) were obtained from Winn Dixie (store brand, 100% juice reconstituted from concentrate).\nCaco-2 cells (a transformed human colon epithelial cell line extensively used to model drug permeability in the intestine [[20](#R20)]) were obtained from ATCC (HTB-37). Cells were cultured at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO_2_. For expansion, cells were cultured in MEM containing Earle's BSS with sodium bicarbonate, 1 g/L glucose, 1 mM sodium pyruvate, 1X nonessential amino acids, 1X penicillin / streptomycin / glutamine, and 20% fetal bovine serum. For transport experiments, sub-confluent Caco-2 cells were trypsinized and then plated at 8.2 × 10^4^ cells/cm^2^ on either MultiScreen Caco-2 plates (Millipore, treated polycarbonate, 0.4 µM pore, 0.11 cm^2^, 96 well, 8 wells per data point), or ThinCert Cell Culture Inserts (Greiner, treated polyethylene terephthalate, 0.4 µm pore, 0.336 cm^2^, 24 well, 5 wells per data point; 4.25 cm^2^, 6 well, 3 wells per data point) in DMEM with sodium bicarbonate, 4.5 g/L glucose, 1X nonessential amino acids, 1X penicillin / streptomycin / glutamine, 10% Fetal bovine serum, and 10 mM HEPES pH 7.5. Cells were allowed to become confluent and differentiate over the subsequent 21 days. Culture media was replaced every 4 days at initial plating; the frequency increasing to every other day as the cells approached confluence.\nMDCKII cells were cultured in MEM containing Earle's BSS with sodium bicarbonate, 1 g/L glucose, 1X penicillin / streptomycin / glutamine and 10% fetal bovine serum. An MDCKII cell line stably expressing OATP2B1 was constructed by calcium phosphate mediated transfection of the OATP2B1 cDNA in pcDNA3.1(+) [[21](#R21)]. Transfectants were selected and expanded in MDCKII medium containing 800 µg/ml G418. For transport experiments, sub-confluent cells resistant to G418 following one month of selection or the parental MDCKII line were trypsinized and then plated at 4.6 × 10^3^ cells/cm^2^ in MDCKII medium with or without G418 as appropriate. Inserts were grown to confluence over the subsequent 3 days. 24 h prior to analysis, transporter expression was induced with 10 mM sodium butyrate [[22](#R22)].\n### Transport experiments\nTransport experiments were conducted in HBSS with CaCl_2_, MgCl_2_, and 1 g/L glucose, buffered at the appropriate pH by addition of either 10 mM MES (pH 5.0–6.5) or 10 mM HEPES (pH 7.0–8.0). In addition, 4% BSA was added to the buffer of the receiver reservoir [[23](#R23)]. Donor and receiver transport buffers containing the indicated drugs were prepared and equilibrated at the appropriate temperature for 30 minutes. When [^3^H]- montelukast was present, it was added to 16.7 µCi/mL. Apical and basolateral reservoirs of confluent and differentiated transport plates or tissue culture inserts were washed three times with the appropriate transport buffer without drug additions and were equilibrated in the final wash at the appropriate temperature for 15 minutes. Transport experiments were initiated by aspiration of the apical buffer and relocation of the cell carrier to the preequilibrated basolateral buffer reservoir followed by addition of the pre-equilibrated apical buffer. Unless otherwise indicated, the donor and receiver reservoirs were associated with the apical and basolateral surfaces, respectively. Incubation was continued at the indicated temperature for the indicated time. In total three samples were taken for liquid scintillation counting: donor reservoir load and donor and receiver reservoir post-transport. Cell layer integrity was assessed post-transport by Lucifer yellow permeability exclusion as previously described [[24](#R24)].","is_error":false}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01UdWWP6djz4H86YdYYju1dw","session_id":"4c2a3f2b-0408-4939-a2aa-89cf838c713d","uuid":"48a7a4c0-a043-4087-beea-84958bc2af5f"}

CYP2C9 is not studied or mentioned as a subject of the research.\n\n---\n\n### 5. PMC5818817.md\n**Relevant: YES**\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Major bleeding\n- Venous thromboembolism (VTE)\n- Supratherapeutic INR (INR ≥ 4)\n- Death\n- Hip or knee arthroplasty (surgical setting)\n- Anticoagulation control\n\n**CYP2C9 variants directly studied:** *1, *2, *3 (genotyped as part of the GIFT randomized clinical trial using genotype-guided dosing). The trial used *CYP2C9*2* and *CYP2C9*3* SNPs alongside VKORC1 and CYP4F2 to guide dosing; genotype-guided dosing significantly reduced adverse events compared to clinically guided dosing.\n\n---\n\n### 8. PMC5859345.md\n**Relevant: YES**\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Warfarin stable dose prediction\n- Anticoagulation (INR target range)\n- Thromboembolic disorders (general indication for warfarin)\n\n**CYP2C9 variants directly studied:** *1, *2 (the study evaluated *2 and *3 in Palestinian patients; *3 allele was not detected in the cohort, frequency = 0%). *CYP2C9*2* had a frequency of 13.6%. The IWPC pharmacogenetic algorithm incorporating *2 and *3 outperformed clinical and fixed-dose algorithms.\n\n---\n\n### 9. The paper uses CYP2C9 star alleles (*2, *3, *5, *8, *11 combined) as covariates in regression models examining warfarin dose, while identifying a novel VKORC1 eQTL (rs4889606) as independently associated with increased dose requirement.\n\n---\n\n### 10. PMC6021962.md\n**Relevant: NO**\n\nThis paper attempts to validate 44 SNPs associated with **tacrolimus** trough concentrations in kidney allograft recipients. Only CYP3A4 and CYP3A5 variants were validated. CYP2C9 variants (*1–*11) are not studied or relevant to this paper's subject matter.\n\n---\n\n### 11. The paper explicitly builds the CPMC-WD dosing table incorporating nine variants including CYP2C9 *1, *2, *3, *5, *6, *8, *11, *14; VKORC1-1639G>A; and CYP4F2 V433M. Genotype combinations covering all these alleles are tabulated and validated.\n\n---\n\n### 12. The paper's primary focus is on nuclear receptor gene (VDR) variants as additional predictors of warfarin dose beyond CYP2C9 and VKORC1.\n\n---\n\n### 13. PMC6714673.md\n**Relevant: YES**\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Warfarin stable maintenance dose variability\n- Over-anticoagulation (supratherapeutic INR)\n- Thromboembolic conditions (general indication for warfarin)\n\n**CYP2C9 variants directly studied:** *1, *2, *3. Genotyped in Indian patients; *2 and *3 were both significant predictors of warfarin dose and risk of over-anticoagulation. Together CYP2C9 *2 and *3 explained 42% of dose variability in the final model.\n\n---\n\n### 14. PMC7347085.md\n**Relevant: YES**\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin (specifically S-warfarin pharmacokinetics)\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- S-warfarin clearance (pharmacokinetics)\n- Warfarin dosing accuracy in African Americans\n- Supratherapeutic INR / bleeding risk (implications of dosing error)\n- Thromboembolic disorders (DVT/PE as indication)\n\n**CYP2C9 variants directly studied:** *1, *2, *3, *5, *6, *8, *11. All were genotyped in 60 African American patients. *CYP2C9*8* was the sole CYP2C9 variant that was a significant independent predictor of S-warfarin clearance (30% reduction in CL(S)), while *2, *3, *5, *6, and *11 did not reach significance, likely due to sample size limitations. The paper demonstrates that algorithms excluding *8 lead to 1.4-fold higher plasma concentrations in *8 carriers.\n\n---\n\n## Summary Table\n\n| # | Filename | Relevant | Drug(s) | Key CYP2C9 Variants | Phenotypes/Conditions |\n|---|----------|----------|---------|---------------------|----------------------|\n| 1 | PMC4513254.md | YES | Warfarin | *1, *2, *3, *5, *6, *8, *11 | Warfarin dose variability, VTE, atrial fibrillation, stroke, bleeding, CKD |\n| 2 | PMC4706412.md | YES | Warfarin | *1, *2, *3, *4, *5, *6, *8, *11 | Warfarin dose requirements, atrial fibrillation, thromboembolism |\n| 3 | PMC5006145.md | YES | Warfarin | *1, *3 | Warfarin maintenance dose variability, thromboembolism |\n| 4 | PMC5369180.md | NO | — | — | — |\n| 5 | PMC5508045.md | YES | Warfarin | *1, *3 | Warfarin stable dose, atrial fibrillation, valve replacement, DVT, PE |\n| 6 | PMC5729933.md | NO | — | — | — |\n| 7 | PMC5818817.md | YES | Warfarin | *1, *2, *3 | Major bleeding, VTE, supratherapeutic INR, death, arthroplasty |\n| 8 | PMC5859345.md | YES | Warfarin | *1, *2 | Warfarin stable dose, anticoagulation, thromboembolism |\n| 9 | PMC5862636.md | YES | Warfarin | *1, *2, *3, *5, *8, *11 | Warfarin dose variability, DVT, PE, anticoagulation |\n| 10 | PMC6021962.md | NO | — | — | — |\n| 11 | PMC6375065.md | YES | Warfarin | *1, *2, *3, *5, *6, *8, *11 | Warfarin dosing categories, over/under-anticoagulation, adverse drug reactions |\n| 12 | PMC6462825.md | YES | Warfarin | *1, *2, *3 | Warfarin dose variability, atrial fibrillation, thromboembolism |\n| 13 | PMC6714673.md | YES | Warfarin | *1, *2, *3 | Warfarin maintenance dose, over-anticoagulation, thromboembolism |\n| 14 | PMC7347085.md | YES | Warfarin | *1, *2, *3, *5, *6, *8, *11 | S-warfarin clearance/PK, dosing accuracy, supratherapeutic INR, DVT/PE |\n\n**11 of 14 papers are relevant** to CYP2C9 variants (*1, *2, *3, *4, *5, *6, *8, *11). PMC5006145.md\n**Relevant: YES**\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Warfarin maintenance dose variability\n- Anticoagulant/antithrombotic therapy\n- Thromboembolic conditions (general, as indication for warfarin)\n\n**CYP2C9 variants directly studied:** *1, *3 (the study focuses specifically on CYP2C9 *1/*1, *1/*3, and *3/*3 genotypes in Han-Chinese patients; *2 is noted as rare/absent in East Asians). Demonstrates *3/*3 requires 72% lower dose, *1/*3 requires 28% lower dose than *1/*1.\n\n---\n\n### 4. CYP2C9 is not studied or mentioned as a subject of the research.\n\n---\n\n### 5. This is the largest prospective warfarin cohort (WARG, n=1496 Swedish patients) demonstrating that CYP2C9 *2 and *3 explain 12% of warfarin dose variance, and that homozygosity for *3 greatly increases risk of INR peaks >4 and severe bleeding.\n\n---\n\n### 13. PMC2630264.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Thromboembolic disease / stroke / venous thromboembolism\n- Over-anticoagulation (INR > 3 and > 4)\n- Severe bleeding / bleeding complications\n- Anticoagulation stability / time in therapeutic range\n- Cardiovascular disease\n\n**CYP2C9 variants specifically discussed:** *1, *2 (rs1799853, R144C), *3 (rs1057910, I359L) — all diplotype combinations (*1/*1, *1/*2, *1/*3, *2/*2, *2/*3, *3/*3). This is the largest prospective warfarin cohort (WARG, n=1496 Swedish patients) demonstrating that CYP2C9 *2 and *3 explain 12% of warfarin dose variance, and that homozygosity for *3 greatly increases risk of INR peaks >4 and severe bleeding.\n\n---\n\n### 13. PMC2641037.md\n**Relevant: No**\nThis paper studies the transporter-mediated absorption of montelukast and the role of OATP2B1 (SLCO2B1) gene variants in montelukast pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics in asthma patients. The gene studied is SLCO2B1 (encoding OATP2B1), not CYP2C9. No CYP2C9 variants are mentioned.\n\n---\n\n### 14. PMC2652833.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Stroke, thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, coronary malfunction (indications for warfarin)\n- Over-anticoagulation (INR > 4.0)\n- Bleeding (severe bleeding risk)\n\n**CYP2C9 variants specifically discussed:** *2 (rs1799853, Arg144Cys) and *3 (rs1057910, Ile359Leu). This GWAS of 1,053 Swedish patients confirms VKORC1, CYP2C9 (*2 and *3), and CYP4F2 as the three principal genetic determinants of warfarin dose, with CYP2C9 *2 explaining ~3.8% and *3 explaining ~8.0% of dose variance independently.\n\n---\n\n## Summary Table\n\n| # | Filename | Relevant | Variants Mentioned | Drugs | Phenotypes/Conditions |\n|---|----------|----------|--------------------|-------|----------------------|\n| 1 | PMC10385908.md | No | — | — | — |\n| 2 | PMC11049954.md | No | — | — | — |\n| 3 | PMC11430620.md | No | — | — | — |\n| 4 | PMC12167464.md | No | — | — | — |\n| 5 | PMC12300161.md | No | — | — | — |\n| 6 | PMC1237155.md | **Yes** | *1, *2, *3 | Warfarin | Thromboembolic disease, atrial arrhythmia, valvuloplasty, arthroplasty, bleeding |\n| 7 | PMC1855340.md | **Yes** | *1, *2, *3 | Warfarin | Thrombosis, cardiovascular disease, orthopedic surgery, valve replacement, atrial fibrillation |\n| 8 | PMC1874262.md | **Yes** | *2, *3 | Warfarin | Warfarin dose requirements, bleeding/over-anticoagulation |\n| 9 | PMC1975838.md | **Yes** | *2, *3 | Warfarin | Total hip/knee replacement, anticoagulation, bleeding risk |\n| 10 | PMC2515139.md | **Yes** | *2, *3 | Warfarin | Anticoagulation, atrial fibrillation, stroke, thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, bleeding |\n| 11 | PMC2564574.md | **Yes** | *2, *3 | Warfarin | Atrial fibrillation, VTE, prosthetic heart valves, anticoagulation |\n| 12 | PMC2630264.md | **Yes** | *1, *2, *3 | Warfarin | VTE, stroke, over-anticoagulation, severe bleeding, anticoagulation stability |\n| 13 | PMC2641037.md | No | — | — | — |\n| 14 | PMC2652833.md | **Yes** | *2, *3 | Warfarin | Stroke, thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, coronary malfunction, over-anticoagulation, bleeding |\n\n**Key finding:** 7 of 14 papers are relevant to CYP2C9 variants. PMC3384479.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Vitamin K antagonists in children with heart disease: height and VKORC1 genotype are the main determinants of the warfarin dose requirement\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin, fluindione (vitamin K antagonists)\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Cardiac disease (cardiac valve replacement, total cavopulmonary connection, dilated cardiomyopathy, Kawasaki disease with coronary aneurysms)\n- Thromboembolism (prevention)\n- Warfarin dose variability; reduced dose requirement with CYP2C9 variant alleles (*2 and *3 specifically genotyped); supratherapeutic INR / overanticoagulation risk\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *1 (wild-type), *2 (rs1799853), *3 (rs1057910). Variant alleles grouped as *1/*x and *x/*x, accounting for 2.0% of warfarin dose variability in children.\n\n---\n\n### 2. PMC3629689.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** CYP2C9 promoter region single-nucleotide polymorphisms linked to the R150H polymorphism are functional suggesting their role in CYP2C9*8-mediated effects\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin (S-warfarin specifically)\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Decreased warfarin clearance (S-warfarin)\n- Lower warfarin dose requirements\n- Risk of supratherapeutic anticoagulation and hemorrhage\n- Reduced CYP2C9 mRNA expression / decreased promoter activity (mechanistic)\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *8 (c.449G>A, R150H, rs7900194) is the primary focus. Also references *2 and *6. Promoter SNPs c.-1766T>C and c.-1188T>C shown to be in linkage disequilibrium with *8 and to synergistically reduce CYP2C9 expression and warfarin clearance.\n\n---\n\n### 3. PMC3718044.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Ability of VKORC1 and CYP2C9 to Predict Therapeutic Warfarin Dose During the Initial Weeks of Therapy\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Idiopathic venous thromboembolism (VTE) / recurrent VTE prevention\n- Supratherapeutic INR / over-anticoagulation (hemorrhage risk)\n- Subtherapeutic INR / under-anticoagulation (thrombosis risk)\n- Warfarin dose variability during initiation of therapy\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *1 (wild-type), *2 (rs1799853), *3 (rs1057910). These were significant independent predictors of therapeutic dose; each variant allele tripled the odds of over-anticoagulation during the first weeks of therapy.\n\n---\n\n### 4. PMC3753327.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Warfarin Anticoagulant Therapy: A Southern Italy Pharmacogenetics-Based Dosing Model\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Cardiovascular diseases (cardiac valve replacement, atrial fibrillation)\n- Bleeding risk / hemorrhage\n- Thromboembolic events (stroke)\n- Warfarin dose variability; reduced dose requirements with CYP2C9 *2 (3.5% variance) and *3 (11.8% variance) alleles\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *1 (wild-type), *2 (rs1799853, p.Arg144Cys), *3 (rs1057910, p.Ile359Leu). These were the only CYP2C9 variants genotyped. Combined with VKORC1 and non-genetic factors, explained 58.4% of dose variability.\n\n---\n\n### 5. PMC3865618.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Association of Genetic Polymorphisms with Warfarin Dose Requirements in Chinese Patients\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Chronic atrial fibrillation, deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, recurrent stroke, prosthetic heart valves\n- Bleeding risk\n- Warfarin dose variability; higher doses required with CYP2C9 rs1057910 AA genotype (wild-type) vs. CC genotype in Chinese population\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** CYP2C9 rs1057910 (*3 locus) was the only CYP2C9 SNP found significant. CYP2C9*2 (rs1799853) was absent in Chinese patients. Note: the paper's reported direction of effect (AA requiring higher doses) reflects the Chinese allele frequency context where rs1057910 AA is the wild-type and CC is the reduced-function variant.\n\n---\n\n### 6. PMC3879795.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Decreased Warfarin Clearance with the CYP2C9 R150H (*8) Polymorphism\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin (S-warfarin and R-warfarin enantiomers)\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Decreased S-warfarin clearance (~30% reduction in unbound oral clearance)\n- Lower warfarin dose requirements (in African American patients)\n- Reduced in vitro intrinsic clearance of S-warfarin 7-hydroxylation\n- Risk of supratherapeutic anticoagulation / hemorrhage (implied)\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *8 (R150H, c.449G>A, rs7900194) is the sole subject. Compared against *1 homozygotes. Demonstrates pharmacokinetic mechanism for *8-associated dose reduction.\n\n---\n\n### 7. PMC3894627.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Genetic Determinants of Response to Warfarin during Initial Anticoagulation\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Various indications for anticoagulation (atrial fibrillation, venous thromboembolism, valve replacement, etc.)\n- Time to first therapeutic INR; time to first INR >4 (supratherapeutic)\n- Time above therapeutic INR range\n- Warfarin dose requirement during and after initiation\n- Bleeding events (major and minor)\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *1 (wild-type), *2 (rs1799853, 430C>T), *3 (rs1057910, 1075A>C), and *5 (rs28371686, 1080C>G) were genotyped. *5 was not found in this population. *2 and *3 carriers reached supratherapeutic INR significantly earlier. VKORC1 haplotype A vs. non-A also central to the study.\n\n---\n\n### 8. PMC3984158.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Verification of Pharmacogenetics-Based Warfarin Dosing Algorithms in Han-Chinese Patients Undertaking Mechanic Heart Valve Replacement\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Mechanical heart valve replacement (anticoagulation management)\n- Warfarin dose prediction accuracy; percentage of patients within 20% of actual therapeutic dose\n- Warfarin dose variability in Han-Chinese patients\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** CYP2C9*3 (rs1057910) is the primary CYP2C9 variant referenced, consistent with the Chinese population focus. The algorithms evaluated incorporated CYP2C9 and VKORC1 SNPs. *1 (wild-type) implicitly referenced as the comparator. CYP2C9*2 is rare/absent in this population.\n\n---\n\n### 9. PMC4043918.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** The impact of age and CYP2C9 and VKORC1 variants on stable warfarin dose in the paediatric population\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Various pediatric indications for anticoagulation (cardiac and non-cardiac)\n- Reduced warfarin dose requirement with any variant CYP2C9 allele (71% of wild-type dose)\n- Warfarin dose variability in children; age-VKORC1 interaction suggesting developmental ontogeny influences warfarin sensitivity\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *1 (wild-type) vs. any variant CYP2C9 allele (grouped). Specific alleles not individually named in the abstract but the paper genotyped for CYP2C9 variant alleles and VKORC1. Variant CYP2C9 alleles collectively accounted for a significant portion of the 53% explained dose variability (alongside age, VKORC1, and age:VKORC1 interaction).\n\n---\n\n### 10. PMC4134280.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** A Pharmacogenetics-Based Warfarin Maintenance Dosing Algorithm from Northern Chinese Patients\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Heart valve replacement (long-term warfarin maintenance)\n- Warfarin dose variability; CYP2C9*3 accounted for 7.0% of dose variability in Northern Chinese Han patients\n- Comorbidities influencing dose: diabetes mellitus, amiodarone use, digoxin use\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *3 (rs1057910) was the only CYP2C9 variant significantly associated with warfarin dose in this Chinese population. *1 (wild-type) is the comparator. *2 is rare in Chinese populations and not found significant. VKORC1 rs7294 was the dominant predictor (27.3%).\n\n---\n\n### 11. PMC4168390.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Characterizing variability in warfarin dose requirements in children using modelling and simulation\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Various pediatric indications for anticoagulation (thrombosis, cardiac conditions)\n- Warfarin dose variability in children (163 children, 6445 INR observations)\n- CYP2C9 genotype explains up to 4-fold difference in dose (*1/*1 vs. *3/*3)\n- VKORC1 genotype explains up to 2-fold difference in dose (G/G vs. A/A)\n- Age, bodyweight, baseline/target INR, time since initiation all significant\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *1 (wild-type), *3 specifically named. Also references *2 in the broader analysis. Genotype effects in children were comparable to adults. CYP4F2 genotype was not significant in this pediatric model.\n\n---\n\n### 12. PMC4209173.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Pharmacogenetics of warfarin in a paediatric population: Time in therapeutic range, initial and stable dosing, and adverse effects\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Congenital heart disease (Fontan's procedure and other cardiac surgeries; 85/97 patients)\n- Non-cardiac indications (12 patients)\n- INR values exceeding target range during initiation (CYP2C9*2 carriers ~4x more likely)\n- Reduced stable warfarin dose requirement with *2 and VKORC1-1639 variants\n- Mild bleeding/hemorrhagic complications (associated with VKORC1-1639 heterozygosity)\n- Proportion of time in therapeutic INR range\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *2 (rs1799853) and *3 (rs1057910) were genotyped. *2 was the primary CYP2C9 finding (significant for INR exceedance in week 1 and stable dose). *3 showed a trend toward lower dose (p=0.049 for stable dose) but limited power due to absence of *3/*3 homozygotes. *1 is the wild-type comparator.\n\n---\n\n### 13. PMC4296935.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Warfarin Dosage Response Related Pharmacogenetics in Chinese Population\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Cardiac valve replacement\n- Warfarin dose variability; reduced maintenance dose with CYP2C9 rs1057910 mutant heterozygote (AC vs. AA wild-type in Chinese)\n- VKORC1 rs9923231 AA and rs7294 CC associated with lower maintenance dose\n- Plasma warfarin concentration differences by genotype\n- rs9923231 and rs1057910 together explain ~32% of dose variability\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** rs1057910 (*3 locus) only; *1 (wild-type) as comparator. CYP2C9*2 (rs1799853) absent in Chinese patients and not genotyped. CYP4F2 and GGCX variants also studied but not significantly associated with dose.\n\n---\n\n### 14. PMC4441275.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Comparative performance of gene-based warfarin dosing algorithms in a multiethnic population\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Multiethnic patient population on anticoagulation (Caucasian, African-American, Hispanic, Asian)\n- Various indications for warfarin (INR goal 2-3)\n- Warfarin dose variability; gene-based algorithms explain 37-55% of variance\n- CYP2C9*5 identified in two African-American/Hispanic patients (not included in any existing algorithm at the time)\n- VKORC1 p.V66M associated with extreme warfarin resistance (~165 mg/week) in one African-American patient\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *1 (wild-type), *2, *3, *4, *5, *6 were all genotyped using the Tag-It kit. *11 was also genotyped separately. *8 is referenced in a cited publication (Scott et al., 2009). The paper specifically discusses *5 and *6 as clinically important variants in non-Caucasian populations that are absent from most published dosing algorithms. CYP2C9*5 carriers were misclassified as *1 in algorithm predictions. This paper is notable for being one of the few in this set to explicitly genotype for *4, *5, *6, and *11.\n\n---\n\n## Summary Table\n\n| Filename | Relevant | Drugs Mentioned | CYP2C9 Variants Addressed | Phenotypes/Conditions |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| PMC3384479.md | Yes | Warfarin, fluindione | *1, *2, *3 | Cardiac disease (pediatric), thromboembolism, warfarin dose variability, overanticoagulation |\n| PMC3629689.md | Yes | Warfarin (S-warfarin) | *8 (primary focus), *2, *6 (referenced) | Decreased warfarin clearance, lower dose requirement, hemorrhage risk (African Americans) |\n| PMC3718044.md | Yes | Warfarin | *1, *2, *3 | Idiopathic VTE, supratherapeutic/subtherapeutic INR, hemorrhage risk |\n| PMC3753327.md | Yes | Warfarin | *1, *2, *3 | Cardiovascular disease, cardiac valve replacement, atrial fibrillation, bleeding, warfarin dose variability |\n| PMC3865618.md | Yes | Warfarin | *3 (rs1057910; PMC3894627.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Genetic Determinants of Response to Warfarin during Initial Anticoagulation\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Various indications for anticoagulation (atrial fibrillation, venous thromboembolism, valve replacement, etc.)\n- Time to first therapeutic INR; time to first INR >4 (supratherapeutic)\n- Time above therapeutic INR range\n- Warfarin dose requirement during and after initiation\n- Bleeding events (major and minor)\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *1 (wild-type), *2 (rs1799853, 430C>T), *3 (rs1057910, 1075A>C), and *5 (rs28371686, 1080C>G) were genotyped. *5 was not found in this population. *2 and *3 carriers reached supratherapeutic INR significantly earlier. VKORC1 haplotype A vs. non-A also central to the study.\n\n---\n\n### 8. PMC3984158.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Verification of Pharmacogenetics-Based Warfarin Dosing Algorithms in Han-Chinese Patients Undertaking Mechanic Heart Valve Replacement\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Mechanical heart valve replacement (anticoagulation management)\n- Warfarin dose prediction accuracy; percentage of patients within 20% of actual therapeutic dose\n- Warfarin dose variability in Han-Chinese patients\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** CYP2C9*3 (rs1057910) is the primary CYP2C9 variant referenced, consistent with the Chinese population focus. The algorithms evaluated incorporated CYP2C9 and VKORC1 SNPs. *1 (wild-type) implicitly referenced as the comparator. CYP2C9*2 is rare/absent in this population.\n\n---\n\n### 9. PMC4043918.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** The impact of age and CYP2C9 and VKORC1 variants on stable warfarin dose in the paediatric population\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Various pediatric indications for anticoagulation (cardiac and non-cardiac)\n- Reduced warfarin dose requirement with any variant CYP2C9 allele (71% of wild-type dose)\n- Warfarin dose variability in children; age-VKORC1 interaction suggesting developmental ontogeny influences warfarin sensitivity\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *1 (wild-type) vs. any variant CYP2C9 allele (grouped). Specific alleles not individually named in the abstract but the paper genotyped for CYP2C9 variant alleles and VKORC1. Variant CYP2C9 alleles collectively accounted for a significant portion of the 53% explained dose variability (alongside age, VKORC1, and age:VKORC1 interaction).\n\n---\n\n### 10. PMC4134280.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** A Pharmacogenetics-Based Warfarin Maintenance Dosing Algorithm from Northern Chinese Patients\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Heart valve replacement (long-term warfarin maintenance)\n- Warfarin dose variability; CYP2C9*3 accounted for 7.0% of dose variability in Northern Chinese Han patients\n- Comorbidities influencing dose: diabetes mellitus, amiodarone use, digoxin use\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *3 (rs1057910) was the only CYP2C9 variant significantly associated with warfarin dose in this Chinese population. *1 (wild-type) is the comparator. *2 is rare in Chinese populations and not found significant. VKORC1 rs7294 was the dominant predictor (27.3%).\n\n---\n\n### 11. PMC4168390.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Characterizing variability in warfarin dose requirements in children using modelling and simulation\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Various pediatric indications for anticoagulation (thrombosis, cardiac conditions)\n- Warfarin dose variability in children (163 children, 6445 INR observations)\n- CYP2C9 genotype explains up to 4-fold difference in dose (*1/*1 vs. *3/*3)\n- VKORC1 genotype explains up to 2-fold difference in dose (G/G vs. A/A)\n- Age, bodyweight, baseline/target INR, time since initiation all significant\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *1 (wild-type), *3 specifically named. Also references *2 in the broader analysis. Genotype effects in children were comparable to adults. CYP4F2 genotype was not significant in this pediatric model.\n\n---\n\n### 12. PMC4209173.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Pharmacogenetics of warfarin in a paediatric population: Time in therapeutic range, initial and stable dosing, and adverse effects\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Congenital heart disease (Fontan's procedure and other cardiac surgeries; 85/97 patients)\n- Non-cardiac indications (12 patients)\n- INR values exceeding target range during initiation (CYP2C9*2 carriers ~4x more likely)\n- Reduced stable warfarin dose requirement with *2 and VKORC1-1639 variants\n- Mild bleeding/hemorrhagic complications (associated with VKORC1-1639 heterozygosity)\n- Proportion of time in therapeutic INR range\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *2 (rs1799853) and *3 (rs1057910) were genotyped. *2 was the primary CYP2C9 finding (significant for INR exceedance in week 1 and stable dose). *3 showed a trend toward lower dose (p=0.049 for stable dose) but limited power due to absence of *3/*3 homozygotes. *1 is the wild-type comparator.\n\n---\n\n### 13. PMC4296935.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Warfarin Dosage Response Related Pharmacogenetics in Chinese Population\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Cardiac valve replacement\n- Warfarin dose variability; reduced maintenance dose with CYP2C9 rs1057910 mutant heterozygote (AC vs. AA wild-type in Chinese)\n- VKORC1 rs9923231 AA and rs7294 CC associated with lower maintenance dose\n- Plasma warfarin concentration differences by genotype\n- rs9923231 and rs1057910 together explain ~32% of dose variability\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** rs1057910 (*3 locus) only; *1 (wild-type) as comparator. CYP2C9*2 (rs1799853) absent in Chinese patients and not genotyped. CYP4F2 and GGCX variants also studied but not significantly associated with dose.\n\n---\n\n### 14. PMC4441275.md\n**Relevant: Yes**\n\n**Title:** Comparative performance of gene-based warfarin dosing algorithms in a multiethnic population\n\n**Drugs mentioned:** Warfarin\n\n**Phenotypes/conditions mentioned:**\n- Multiethnic patient population on anticoagulation (Caucasian, African-American, Hispanic, Asian)\n- Various indications for warfarin (INR goal 2-3)\n- Warfarin dose variability; gene-based algorithms explain 37-55% of variance\n- CYP2C9*5 identified in two African-American/Hispanic patients (not included in any existing algorithm at the time)\n- VKORC1 p.V66M associated with extreme warfarin resistance (~165 mg/week) in one African-American patient\n\n**CYP2C9 variants addressed:** *1 (wild-type), *2, *3, *4, *5, *6 were all genotyped using the Tag-It kit. *11 was also genotyped separately. *8 is referenced in a cited publication (Scott et al., 2009). The paper specifically discusses *5 and *6 as clinically important variants in non-Caucasian populations that are absent from most published dosing algorithms. CYP2C9*5 carriers were misclassified as *1 in algorithm predictions. This paper is notable for being one of the few in this set to explicitly genotype for *4, *5, *6, and *11.\n\n---\n\n## Summary Table\n\n| Filename | Relevant | Drugs Mentioned | CYP2C9 Variants Addressed | Phenotypes/Conditions |\n|---|---|---|---|---|\n| PMC3384479.md | Yes | Warfarin, fluindione | *1, *2, *3 | Cardiac disease (pediatric), thromboembolism, warfarin dose variability, overanticoagulation |\n| PMC3629689.md | Yes | Warfarin (S-warfarin) | *8 (primary focus), *2, *6 (referenced) | Decreased warfarin clearance, lower dose requirement, hemorrhage risk (African Americans) |\n| PMC3718044.md | Yes | Warfarin | *1, *2, *3 | Idiopathic VTE, supratherapeutic/subtherapeutic INR, hemorrhage risk |\n| PMC3753327.md | Yes | Warfarin | *1, *2, *3 | Cardiovascular disease, cardiac valve replacement, atrial fibrillation, bleeding, warfarin dose variability |\n| PMC3865618.md | Yes | Warfarin | *3 (rs1057910; However, *CYP2C9***11* recently has been associated with reduced therapeutic warfarin [[25](#R25)] and acenocoumarol [[35](#R35)] dose requirements. [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clpt.2003.12.017) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15116052/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=Clin%20Pharmacol%20Ther&title=Acenocoumarol%20stabilization%20is%20delayed%20in%20CYP2C93%20carriers&author=T%20Schalekamp&author=JH%20van%20Geest-Daalderop&author=H%20de%20Vries-Goldschmeding&author=J%20Conemans&author=M%20Bernsen%20Mj&volume=75&publication_year=2004&pages=394-402&pmid=15116052&doi=10.1016/j.clpt.2003.12.017&)\n/app/papers/PMC2789741.md:258:Among Europeans, Schalekamp et al reported an increased risk of over-anticoagulation (INR>6) among phenprocoumon users possessing variant *CYP2C9* and/or variant *VKORC1* genotype[[42](#R42)] and among acenocoumarol users possessing both variant *CYP2C9* and variant *VKORC1* genotype.[[43](#R43)] Schelleman et al, Kealey et al and Schwarz et al also reported a significantly increased risk of over-anticoagulation (INR>4) among European American warfarin users possessing variant *VKORC1* genotype after adjustment for *CYP2C9* and clinical covariates.[[26](#R26); [27](#R27); [33](#R33)] Our findings among European Americans concur with these earlier reports. The differences in risk ratios among European American populations in our study and prior studies may perhaps be explained by the number and nature of clinical covariates and the inclusion of a measure of intra-patient variability in INR (unobserved heterogeneity) in our analyses. Inability to account for such heterogeneity has been recognized as a limitation by several investigators. [[27](#R27); [44](#R44)] Our results provide evidence that the gene-over-anticoagulation association is independent of such heterogeneity. The recent prospective randomized study by Anderson et al confirms these gene-over-anticoagulation associations with the excess risk of over-anticoagulation driven mainly by the concurrent possession of *CYP2C9* and *VKORC1* variants. Piquet P, Losay J, Doubine S. Acenocoumarol (Sintrom) and fluindione (Previscan) in pediatrics after cardiac surgical procedures [article in French]. Arch Pediatr. 2002;9(11):1137–1144. doi: 10.1016/s0929-693x(02)00092-1.  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1016/s0929-693x(02)00092-1) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12503504/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=Arch%20Pediatr&title=Acenocoumarol%20(Sintrom)%20and%20fluindione%20(Previscan)%20in%20pediatrics%20after%20cardiac%20surgical%20procedures%20%5Barticle%20in%20French%5D.&author=P%20Piquet&author=J%20Losay&author=S%20Doubine&volume=9&issue=11&publication_year=2002&pages=1137-1144&pmid=12503504&doi=10.1016/s0929-693x(02)00092-1&)\n/app/papers/PMC3894627.md:350:4. Takahashi H, Wilkinson GR, Padrini R, Echizen H. CYP2C9 and oral anticoagulation therapy with acenocoumarol and warfarin: similarities yet differences. Clin Pharmacol Ther. 2004;75:376–380. doi: 10.1016/j.clpt.2004.01.007.  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.clpt.2004.01.007) | [PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15116049/) | [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar_lookup?journal=Clin%20Pharmacol%20Ther&title=CYP2C9%20and%20oral%20anticoagulation%20therapy%20with%20acenocoumarol%20and%20warfarin:%20similarities%20yet%20differences&author=H%20Takahashi&author=GR%20Wilkinson&author=R%20Padrini&author=H%20Echizen&volume=75&publication_year=2004&pages=376-380&pmid=15116049&doi=10.1016/j.clpt.2004.01.007&)\n/app/papers/PMC4134280.md:19:Warfarin has remained the mainstay of oral anticoagulant therapy for the treatment and prevention of thromboembolism. However, management of warfarin therapy is challenging because of its narrow therapeutic index and wide inter-individual variability. The correct dosing is necessary to avoid bleeding or risk of thrombotic events in case of an excessive or a too low dose. Many studies have shown that single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) within *CYP2C9* (cytochrome P450, family 2, subfamily C, polypeptide 9) and *VKORC1* (vitamin K epoxide reductase complex, subunit 1) genes are related to warfarin dose requirement [[1]](#pone.0105250-Sconce1), [[2]](#pone.0105250-Rieder1), [[3]](#pone.0105250-Borgiani1), [[4]](#pone.0105250-Carlquist1). These two genes in combination with age, gender, and body mass index have been shown to account for 30–50% of the variability in the dosage of warfarin and acenocoumarol [[1]](#pone.0105250-Sconce1), [[4]](#pone.0105250-Carlquist1), [[5]](#pone.0105250-Teichert1), [[6]](#pone.0105250-DAndrea1), [[7]](#pone.0105250-Geisen1), [[8]](#pone.0105250-Aquilante1), [[9]](#pone.0105250-Oldenburg1), [[10]](#pone.0105250-Wadelius1). In addition to *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9* SNPs, *CYP4F2* (cytochrome P450, family 4, subfamily F, polypeptide 2) rs2108622(V433M) was found to be associated with warfarin dose in 3 independent Caucasian cohorts and accounted for the difference in warfarin dose of approximately 1 mg/day between CC and TT subjects [[11]](#pone.0105250-Caldwell1). Several genome-wide association studies indicated that there seemed to be no common SNPs with large effects on warfarin maintenance dose outside of the *CYP2C9*, *VKORC1*, and *CYP4F2* [[12]](#pone.0105250-Cooper1), [[13]](#pone.0105250-Takeuchi1), [[14]](#pone.0105250-Teichert2), [[15]](#pone.0105250-Cha1). A recent association study with a Chinese population also confirmed this observation [[16]](#pone.0105250-Zhong1). Candidate gene studies have also detected associations between other SNPs and warfarin dose, such as SNPs in *CYP2C19* (cytochrome P450, family 2, subfamily C, polypeptide 19) [[17]](#pone.0105250-Rusdiana1), *GGCX* (gamma-glutamyl carboxylase) [[18]](#pone.0105250-Cha2), [[19]](#pone.0105250-Chan1), [[20]](#pone.0105250-Huang1), [[21]](#pone.0105250-Luxembourg1), *EPHX1* (epoxide hydrolase 1) [[21]](#pone.0105250-Luxembourg1), [[22]](#pone.0105250-Wang1), [[23]](#pone.0105250-Pautas1), [[24]](#pone.0105250-Gu1), *CALU* (calumenin) [[25]](#pone.0105250-Voora1), and *MPZ* (myelin protein zero) [[12]](#pone.0105250-Cooper1). However, the associations of these SNPs with warfarin dose were inconsistent. In *CYP4F2* gene, a functional haplotype block represented by rs3093105 was found to be associated with urinary 20-hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acid (20-HETE) and hypertension in Chinese individuals [[26]](#pone.0105250-Liu1), while rs2074902 in the haplotype block and rs3093158 were found to be associated with Crohn's disease [[27]](#pone.0105250-Costea1). We also included some functional SNPs in related genes including *F2* (coagulation factor II), *F5* (coagulation factor V), *F11* (coagulation factor XI), and *PROCR* (protein C receptor), whose proteins are involved in thrombosis. Previous studies constructed many algorithms to predict the warfarin maintenance dose in Chinese individuals [[16]](#pone.0105250-Zhong1), [[28]](#pone.0105250-Xu1), [[29]](#pone.0105250-You1), [[30]](#pone.0105250-Zhang1), [[31]](#pone.0105250-Huang2), [[32]](#pone.0105250-Wei1). However, they frequently used relatively small populations (<400 subjects) except in two studies where one included 845 from Southern China [[16]](#pone.0105250-Zhong1), and the other included 641 from Central China [[33]](#pone.0105250-Tan1). The two algorithms included different factors and could explain the 43.6% [[16]](#pone.0105250-Zhong1) and 56.4% [[33]](#pone.0105250-Tan1) variability in warfarin maintenance dose, respectively. The objective of the current study was to assess these genetic determinants of the warfarin maintenance dose and to construct an algorithm integrating common interference factors to predict the dose in a large population who lived in Northern China.\n/app/papers/PMC4134280.md:278:5.
